tossups.question: This place is contrasted with a “world inhabited by civilized Cartesian phantoms” in a book’s opening paragraph. A collection set in this place, meant to be worthy of a “world, chiseled out of stone,” features the author bidding “Farewell” to his girlfriend Maria. A story’s narrator says, “I am not a Count of Monte Cristo” upon recognizing a doctor from this place based on how he spells “naphthenate” (“NAFF-thuh-nate”) in a letter about faulty varnish. Autobiographical stories about Tadek set in this place are quoted in the chapter “Beta, the Disappointed Lover” from The Captive Mind. To teach Italian to Jean, a man in this place recites Inferno’s “Canto of Ulysses” from memory. A man trades goods stolen in this place for bread in “Cerium,” a story in The Periodic Table. For 10 points, Primo Levi’s memoir If This is a Man depicts the horrors of what place?
tossups.question_sanitized: This place is contrasted with a "world inhabited by civilized Cartesian phantoms" in a book's opening paragraph. A collection set in this place, meant to be worthy of a "world, chiseled out of stone," features the author bidding "Farewell" to his girlfriend Maria. A story's narrator says, "I am not a Count of Monte Cristo" upon recognizing a doctor from this place based on how he spells "naphthenate" ("NAFF-thuh-nate") in a letter about faulty varnish. Autobiographical stories about Tadek set in this place are quoted in the chapter "Beta, the Disappointed Lover" from The Captive Mind. To teach Italian to Jean, a man in this place recites Inferno's "Canto of Ulysses" from memory. A man trades goods stolen in this place for bread in "Cerium," a story in The Periodic Table. For 10 points, Primo Levi's memoir If This is a Man depicts the horrors of what place?
tossups.answer: Auschwitz [accept Oświęcim or Auschwitz II-Birkenau or Auschwitz III-Monowitz; accept Survival in Auschwitz; prompt on concentration camps or death camps or Konzentrationslager] (The third sentence is from “Vanadium.” The Captive Mind is by Czesław Miłosz. The author is Tadeusz Borowski.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Auschwitz [accept Oswiecim or Auschwitz II-Birkenau or Auschwitz III-Monowitz; accept Survival in Auschwitz; prompt on concentration camps or death camps or Konzentrationslager] (The third sentence is from "Vanadium." The Captive Mind is by Czeslaw Milosz. The author is Tadeusz Borowski.)

tossups.question: This poet asserted “it’s possible to conjecture that mankind exists / To provision and populate Hell” in an ekphrastic poem describing Bosch’s Garden of Earthly Delights. This poet wrote of “trees outside the window, which probably exist” in a poem opening “We are not so badly off, if we can / Admire Dutch painting.” Another poem by this author of the poem “Realism” mentions a fisherman mending “a glimmering net” and a bee circling a clover before closing with the repeated line “There will be no other (*) end of the world.” This author’s 1995 collection Facing the River was translated into English by his colleague Robert Hass. An anti-communist book by this author discusses the ideologies of men nicknamed Alpha and Beta. For 10 points, name this dissident Polish poet who wrote The Captive Mind.
tossups.question_sanitized: This poet asserted "it's possible to conjecture that mankind exists / To provision and populate Hell" in an ekphrastic poem describing Bosch's Garden of Earthly Delights. This poet wrote of "trees outside the window, which probably exist" in a poem opening "We are not so badly off, if we can / Admire Dutch painting." Another poem by this author of the poem "Realism" mentions a fisherman mending "a glimmering net" and a bee circling a clover before closing with the repeated line "There will be no other (*) end of the world." This author's 1995 collection Facing the River was translated into English by his colleague Robert Hass. An anti-communist book by this author discusses the ideologies of men nicknamed Alpha and Beta. For 10 points, name this dissident Polish poet who wrote The Captive Mind.

tossups.question: A poem in this language notes that a “white-haired old man” who “binds his tomatoes” is the only one who believes that the title event “is happening now.” Another poem in this language describes those who “push the rubble to the side of the road” and “get mired in scum and ashes.” A “Treatise on Poetry” in this language was translated into English by Robert Hass. A poem in this language reflects that “after every war, someone has to (*) clean up” and is titled “The End and The Beginning.” “Delta, the Troubadour” appears in a book about this language’s literature titled The Captive Mind. A novel in this language follows a patrician who falls in love with Lygia and is set during “the time of Nero.” For 10 points, Czesław Miłosz (“CHESS-wahf MEE-wosh”) and the author of Quo Vadis, Henryk Sienkiewicz (“shen-KYEH-vich”), wrote in what language?
tossups.answer: Polish [or Polski] (The first poem is “Song on the End of the World” by Miłosz; “The End and the Beginning” is by Wisława Szymborska.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Polish [or Polski] (The first poem is "Song on the End of the World" by Milosz; "The End and the Beginning" is by Wislawa Szymborska.)

tossups.question: This author’s viewing of the frescoes of Luca Signorelli led him to examine the apocalyptic nature of Vladimir Solovyov’s writings in his essay “Science Fiction and the Coming of Antichrist.” In another essay, this author examined the philosopher Lev Shestov’s “purity of despair.” This man, who examined “modes of eccentric vision” in Emperor of the Earth, wrote a book that examines a writer’s novel about the dictator Murti Bing, who gives everyone a special pill to strip people of their resistance. This man’s poetry collection Unattainable Earth was the first of his collaborations with Robert Hass, who championed him while they were both at Berkeley. In a book about Beta the Disenchanted Lover and Delta the Troubadour, this author wrote about 4 intellectuals who are initially attracted to Communism. For 10 points, name this Polish author of The Captive Mind.
tossups.question_sanitized: This author's viewing of the frescoes of Luca Signorelli led him to examine the apocalyptic nature of Vladimir Solovyov's writings in his essay "Science Fiction and the Coming of Antichrist." In a book about Beta the Disenchanted Lover and Delta the Troubadour, this author wrote about 4 intellectuals who are initially attracted to Communism. For 10 points, name this Polish author of The Captive Mind.

bonuses.leadin: The protagonist of a novel by this author proposes the doing of a “Most Terrible Deed” in order to escape a cellar filled with people who periodically gore each other in the thigh with bent spurs. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author who wrote of Gonzalo’s attempts to both sleep with Ignatio and trick him into murdering his father in a fictionalization of their accidental exile in Argentina, the comic novel Trans-Atlantyk.

In an early entry of the Diary, Gombrowicz responds to this author’s critique of Trans-Atlantyk as irrelevant to a Poland under Marxism. Later in the Diary, Gombrowicz notes his approval of this Polish-American author’s The Captive Mind.

The line “Before us is ‘The Heart of Darkness’” ends this author’s long poem The Treatise on Morality. This editor of The Book of Luminous Things wrote a book that discusses four intellectuals with the aliases Alpha, Beta, Gamma, and Delta.

Conrad is called to “Come back from Kent ... from Westminster!” in a Marian Piechal (“mar-yahn pee-eh-KHALL”) poem titled for this place. Another poem titled for this place advises you to “keep [this place] always in your mind” and claims it “gave you the marvelous journey.”
answers: Joseph Conrad [or Józef Teodor Konrad Korzeniowski; accept “On the death of Joseph Conrad”]
answers_sanitized: Joseph Conrad [or Jozef Teodor Konrad Korzeniowski; accept "On the death of Joseph Conrad"]
Czeslaw Milosz (The book is The Captive Mind.)

parts: Name this language used by the Nobel-Prize-winning poet of the collections Salt and Nothing Twice. Robert Hass translated “A Song on the End of the World” from this language to English.
Cavanagh’s translation of this author’s poem “Try to Praise the Mutilated World” was printed on the back page of The New Yorker after 9/11. This member of Poland’s Generation of ’68 died in 2021.
Cavanagh’s essay “The Art of Losing” pushes back against the desire for literal translations using the example of a Polish translation of this American poet’s villanelle “One Art.”

bonuses.leadin: One work outlines seven forms of “Ketman” that dissidents use to silently protest the policies of this country. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this country whose censorship policies were avoided through unofficial publications called samizdat. This country promoted literature in the socialist realism genre.
The seven forms of “Ketman” Soviet dissidence are discussed in this work, which describes fellow writers from the author’s country in sections like “Alpha, the Moralist.”

In The Captive Mind, Milosz compares the plot of a novel in this genre, Insatiability, to the effects of Marxism-Leninism on the Soviet republics. Yevgeny Zamyatin’s novel We in this genre may have inspired a later novel in this genre contrasted with its author’s later work Island.
answers: Soviet Union [or USSR; accept Union of Soviet Socialist Republics; prompt on Russia]
dystopian novel [accept answers describing dystopias; prompt on futuristic novels or science fiction]

bonuses.leadin: Answer some questions about poetry that draws upon the Beatles, for 10 points each.
parts: A poet in this language lowered the singer’s age to 63 in his adaptation of “When I’m Sixty-Four.” His book Surgical Precision won the Nike Award, the top prize for literature in this language, a year after a Nobel laureate won it for his collection Roadside Dog.

This poet described his sexual awakening “Between the end of the ‘Chatterley’ ban / And the Beatles' first LP” in his poem “Annus Mirabilis.” He wrote: “They fuck you up, your mum and dad” in his poem “This Be the Verse”.
In chapter VI of this long poem, the pregnant housemaid Helen sings the Beatles’ “Yesterday” while strolling down the beach, before imagining the burning of Troy.
answers: Polish [or polski; the poets are Stanisław Barańczak and Czesław Miłosz]
answers_sanitized: Polish [or polski; the poets are Stanislaw Baranczak and Czeslaw Milosz]

bonuses.leadin: A poet from this country created the Everyman alter ego Mr. Cogito during his travels abroad. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this European country home to Zbigniew Herbert. A Nobel laureate from this country who wrote with "ironic precision" lived in its city of Kraków.
The aforementioned Nobel laureate is this poet of the collection Nothing Twice. Every sentence of her poem "Possibilities" begins with "I prefer."
This other Polish Nobel laureate of The Captive Mind wrote "A Treatise on Poetry," which details the literary life and history of Poland before going into its last section set in Pennsylvania.

bonuses.leadin: The “Cricot 2” theater group, founded by avant-garde artist Tadeusz Kantor, was initially dedicated to staging productions of this writer’s plays, such as The Cuttlefish and The Water Hen. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Polish writer and painter, whom Martin Esslin cited as a precursor to the “Theater of the Absurd.” Chinese armies take over Europe in the year 2000 in this author’s novel Insatiability.

The first section of this book uses the “Murti Bing pills” from Insatiability as a metaphor for dogmatic thought. It analyzes the case histories of four intellectuals under the pseudonyms “Alpha,” “Beta,” “Gamma,” and “Delta.”

In The Captive Mind, “Beta,” who represents Tadeusz Borowski, becomes a propagandist for the Polish government after returning from this location. This location is the setting of Primo Levi’s If This is a Man.

Miłosz suggested that “poems should be written rarely and reluctantly, / under unbearable duress” in a poem titled “Ars Poetica” in reference to this Roman poet, who wrote “carpe diem” in one of his books of odes.

parts_sanitized: Name this poet who wrote about Alpha, Beta, Gamma, and Delta in The Captive Mind, which attempts to explain why Stalinism appeals to intellectuals.
Milosz hails from this Eastern European country. The author of Quo Vadis, Henryk Sinkiewicz ("shen-KYEY-vitch"), is from here.
This Irish poet, who wrote about seeing creatures "poised like mud grenades" that created "warm thick slobber" in one work, was heavily influenced by Milosz and read a poem at his funeral.

bonuses.leadin: This poet noted that “it's in [time's] nature not to stay” since “today is always gone tomorrow” in her poem “Nothing Twice.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this poet who wrote that “after every war, someone has to clean up” in her poem “The End and the Beginning”. This author's poem “Love at First Sight” ends with the affirmation that “every beginning is only a sequel, after all, and the book of events is always open halfway through.”

Szymborska won the Nobel Prize in part for a poem from the perspective of one of these animals wandering around an empty apartment. Another poet published a volume titled for Old Possum's Book of Practical these animals.

This fellow Pole who authored an essay on Szymborska notes that people who expect “lightning and thunder” will be disappointed in his poem “A Song on the End of the World.” This author described people such as Alpha, Beta, Gamma, and Delta in his work The Captive Mind.
answers_sanitized: Wislawa Szymborska (Vi-swa-va Shim-bor-ska) [or Maria Wislawa Anna Szymborska-Wlodek]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This poet noted that "it's in [time's] nature not to stay" since "today is always gone tomorrow" in her poem "Nothing Twice." For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this poet who wrote that "after every war, someone has to clean up" in her poem "The End and the Beginning". This author's poem "Love at First Sight" ends with the affirmation that "every beginning is only a sequel, after all, and the book of events is always open halfway through."

Szymborska won the Nobel Prize in part for a poem from the perspective of one of these animals wandering around an empty apartment. Another poet published a volume titled for Old Possum's Book of Practical these animals.

This fellow Pole who authored an essay on Szymborska notes that people who expect "lightning and thunder" will be disappointed in his poem "A Song on the End of the World." This author described people such as Alpha, Beta, Gamma, and Delta in his work The Captive Mind.

bonuses.leadin: This author began his poem “Account” by declaring that “The history of my stupidity would fill many volumes.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author who described his “former lives… departing, / like ships, together with their sorrow” in his poem “Late Ripeness,” which was translated into English with the help of his Berkeley colleague Robert Hass.

Miłosz wrote a poem declaring that these works should be written “rarely and reluctantly, / under unbearable duress.” A similarly-titled poem by Archibald MacLeish states that these writings “should not mean / But be.”
Miłosz reports that a “drunkard grows sleepy at the edge of a lawn” in a poetic “Song on” this event, which a different poem predicts will happen “Not with a bang but with a whimper.”
answers: the end of the world [or descriptive equivalents]

packet.name: B. OSU A + Wisconsin A + Cambridge B

answers_sanitized: the end of the world [or descriptive equivalents]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This author began his poem "Account" by declaring that "The history of my stupidity would fill many volumes." For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this author who described his "former lives... departing, / like ships, together with their sorrow" in his poem "Late Ripeness," which was translated into English with the help of his Berkeley colleague Robert Hass.

Milosz wrote a poem declaring that these works should be written "rarely and reluctantly, / under unbearable duress." A similarly-titled poem by Archibald MacLeish states that these writings "should not mean / But be."
Milosz reports that a "drunkard grows sleepy at the edge of a lawn" in a poetic "Song on" this event, which a different poem predicts will happen "Not with a bang but with a whimper."

bonuses.leadin: In one novel by this author, Marina is seduced by Karlinsky, despite being married to the state prosecutor Globov, who is working on the conviction of Rabinovich, while his own son is convicted of Trotskyite subversion. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author, whose works, like the novels The Trial Begins and The Makepeace Experiment and a critique of Socialist Realism were smuggled out of the USSR and published abroad from 1959 until his arrest and trial in 1965.

In The Trial Begins, Globov initially investigates Rabinovich for performing this action illegally, and becomes estranged from his wife Marina, when she declares that she had the same procedure without his knowledge.
The English edition of Tertz’s critique of Socrealism is often preceded by an essay on the then-anonymous author by this Polish émigré, himself the author of The Captive Mind.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: In one novel by this author, Marina is seduced by Karlinsky, despite being married to the state prosecutor Globov, who is working on the conviction of Rabinovich, while his own son is convicted of Trotskyite subversion. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this author, whose works, like the novels The Trial Begins and The Makepeace Experiment and a critique of Socialist Realism were smuggled out of the USSR and published abroad from 1959 until his arrest and trial in 1965.

In The Trial Begins, Globov initially investigates Rabinovich for performing this action illegally, and becomes estranged from his wife Marina, when she declares that she had the same procedure without his knowledge.
The English edition of Tertz's critique of Socrealism is often preceded by an essay on the then-anonymous author by this Polish emigre, himself the author of The Captive Mind.

bonuses.leadin: In one of this author’s poems, a man points to a hare during a trip through “frozen fields in a wagon at dawn” and the speaker notes that neither the hare nor the man are alive today. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author who wrote collections such as Facing the River and The Light of Day, but is best known for a work of nonfiction in which Alpha, Beta, Gamma, and Delta crumble under a communist regime.

Milosz wrote The Captive Mind while in political exile from this European country, whose historical connection with Lithuania was the subject of Adam Mickiewicz (mee KAY vich)’s epic Pan Tadeusz (ta DOITCH).

An earlier Polish work is this novel by Sienkiewicz (shen KAY vich), about the relationship of the Christian woman Lygia and the Roman patrician Marcus Vicinius in Nero’s regime. Its title is Latin for “where are you going?”
answers: Czeslaw Milosz (mil OSH; like the first syllable in “ocean”)
Quo Vadis: A Narrative of the Time of Nero
